item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Due to this, the first image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 for nice short and sweet body horror without going overly gross.

SCP-1912-B is to be harvested by two D-Class wearing full protective clothing, and stored in airtight drums in refrigerated storage area C.

Their storing D-Class personnel in refrigerated airtight drums!? I know these guys are death-row serial killers and rapists, but that feels a little excessive…

item.title: Image sources
item.content:encoded:
the bees are from https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Honeybee_thermal_defence01.jpg - Creative Commons, attribution required. This statement counts as attribution.

the watch, however - it's a photo of a modern replica of Abraham-Louis Breguet's "Marie-Antoinette" watch, the one that took 44 years to make. The original is lost, the Breguet company made a replica as a showpiece, and has refused to sell it even though they've been offered literally millions of dollars for it. I can't source the photo more precisely than a variety of articles about expensive things, but there's no indication that it's available under a suitable license.

item.title: Rewrite Announcement
item.content:encoded: A rewrite is currently in progress by Rimple.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is there any chance that this would be up for rewrite? Because if so, I would love to take a stab at this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How is this still up? Everytime it starts getting downvoted someone looks at it and goes "Cool" and then everyone starts upvoting?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't think it was too bad, Much better than my article. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bee blisters: Yes!

Bee blisters from a totally unrelated object: No.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to downvote on the principle that it's an item that generates seemingly infinite mass for no discernible reason. The origins of the item seem completely out of step with the effect generated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As a melissophobe, I found this very creepy, so I voted it up (I am going over items "on the verge" to help decide if to delete them).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article has potential, but is kind of confusing (at least to me). No vote, because this really creeps me out (I HATE bees.), and I could see something better come of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The 1912-1, -A, -B distinction is confusing. Might wanna go with -A, -B, -C. The article as a whole reads weirdly. The tone is less than clinical at times, and the structuring could be much better (origin story can go at the end, for example). I like the general idea, but it doesn't grab me in its current form. Reluctant -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe it's my sick obsession with bees, but I personally liked this. It as an air of weirdness that just sort of resonates with me.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Time is honey, people! Chop-chop!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This would be good. It's not terrible and certainly plays well into the bee swam factor, but it ultimately is poorly organized and there's a serious feeling that it wants to go somewhere but ends up staying pretty unmoved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmmmmmmmmmmmmmmmmm…

I could give this a go on a rewrite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a watch…. that makes bees?… I expected to see a lot of things here… but not this. Out of all the weird SCP here at the Foundation this one seems… different… just somehow different…

Up vote… I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So at first it says that the nectar is created on it's own and then later it says the bees are needed for it. Did I miss something?

At least you didnt add random killings for no reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Not the bees!" - Nicolas Cage

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
THE WATCH IS NOT BEEZ

TEH GROSS WATCH STUF GET ON UR SKEN

AND DEN U R BEEZ!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I apologize for necroposting, but how did it take 8 months before someone made this comment?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For me it's a no vote. Overall the writing and concept is pretty good but it's not that appealing to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
[I realize this might be somewhat late.]

It sounds as though the watch continually produces some amount of -A even without the bees present, which could eventually become a problem if there's not some way to dispose of it. Allowing the bees to turn it into -B apparently neutralizes its anomalous properties, so it makes sense to have a colony on hand.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What if you picked up a watch and instead of a watch it was bees?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The execution is good, and the idea is… at least unique.
However I see no reason why it would be a watch? It would make far more sense if the liquid came from some sort of flower or bee's nest…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The real anomaly here is that nobody has asked "What if the watch was bees?"

On the one-hand, this is well-written and does a very good job of meeting the challenge prompts. On the other, we already have at least half a dozen items that spawn swarms of things from the victim. Worse, I can't construct any fridge logic of why even the most deranged eco-nut would think this was a desirable effect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll second this. It might be better to state that the substance is nectar, or that it bears a close resemblance to nectar, rather than claiming it to be identical in every way; the latter phrasing is curiously precise, calling attention to a claim that the scientists here probably don't really have enough information to make.

Between that little oddity and the proliferation of comma splices among other grammatical issues, I can't in good conscience upvote this. Fix the grammar errors, though, and this is thought-provoking enough to earn my +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If there is pollen in it, yes. If not, then not reliably. Nectar composition doesn't follow genetic relatedness as well as we would like, so its not even always good for that. Like flower shape/color, it tends to follow target pollinator instead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And here i thought it was the bee's knees…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
-1.

This feels really disjointed, and I'm sorry to say that the anomalous effect isn't interesting enough to justify the incomprehensible connection between a watch and a swarm of bees…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks to everyone for their help and replies, I'm afraid I posted my first scp during a time when I'm extremely busy with university so don't have time to look too far into the complaints here however if it isn't deleted I will as soon as possible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not bad! Will upvote if you lose the lolFoundation collapsible at the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I suggest you drop the "anomalous bee honey in the foundation staff room" thingy. This is silly, and reeks of lolfoundation

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the writing is pretty good, as is the concept. One minor change might be that, instead of feeding the staff, maybe one of the D-class started sneaking out honey, ultimately causing a minor riot about the plight of bees.

Anyway, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Danke schoen. I had a nagging feeling I'd missed a small detail.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was ok till I opened that collapsible. Not to mention there are some minor issues with the main body.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Frogs, Kitten Flu, and now Bee Blisters. I'm seeing a theme here, and it makes me uncomfortable. Which, I guess, is a reaction worth a response, so +1.

Question, though : Why a watch? Which you answered in your first post.
Don't anybody look at me, I'm a moron.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's mostly sugar water, yes. Still, it's probably entirely possible to identify the species of plant from which any particular nectar came from.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm on a crappy phone at the moment, so I don't have remotely the patience to wait for it to pull up google and search, but I'm reasonably confident that nectar is the term for all manner of sweet liquid one would find in a plant. There are slight changes in every plant(and I mean in literally every individual plant, not just different groups of them) but it's still more or less sugar-water of a manner.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"The fluid is identical in every way to nectar except for its primary anomalous effect."

What kind of nectar? I doubt all plant species secrete identical nectar.

Also, does it have an effect o nany other animals, or just humans?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually quite like this. The whole blister element was… Egad. I started having shivers just from that.

I actually like the whole food element. It kind of makes sense. The effect is very minor, without any obvious physical or psychological effects becoming evident. It took 3 months for it to introduce a relatively minor behavioral change. It's the kind fo thing that could easily seem to slip through the cracks in testing. And it makes sense to me for the honey to be consumed, with the current arrangement. They're going to have tons of honey building up from these things. All their testing indicated it was normal honey. So why not put it to some use. Sort of like the green slime.

One thought i had, which might prove an interesting angle to expand on. What is the reproduction subject is allergic to bees?

Overall, a fun article with some interesting design. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this when it was a draft and I enjoy it now. Although it's not perfect, I feel the positives outweigh the negatives.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not sure if this is your first article submitted, but your writing here is pretty decent, although you do need to watch your grammar and punctuation..

If anything, I'd say this one only has some issues when it comes to the logic employed to utilize all the words you got from the random word generator. If you want the bees to be a part of this then they need to be anomalous. Right now their birth appears to be, but the bees themselves are regular honeybees. There also seems to be little coherence between the watch and the rest of the scip. It feels tacked on right now, like it was the word you were left with at the end. You need to find out how to bring that together.

I see potential here, but there's a bit more work to be done. If you want and need any help, feel free to contact me through Wikidot PM or by a tell in chat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The first thing I do not understand, is why an active colony must be kept around in the first place. The SCP is the watch, not the colony itself. It would be much better to just kill all instances of SCP-1912-1 and just have SCP-1912 in some standard containment locker, which would make it "Safe." If there was no instances of SCP-1912-1, then they could not try to spread the nectar or whatever around to infect more people. The Foundation has no reason to keep any instances of SCP-1912-1, since they could just produce more if they felt the need. This would save a lot of expenses and D-Class as well.

The second thing is that it is very hard to believe that the researchers would allow the honey to be consumed by anyone after a few D-Class said it was good. (Like an above poster mentioned) I believe you were trying to pull off something like SCP-884, but it just did not work and felt more added on than anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Due to this, Dr █████ made SCP-1912-B available for consumption in the staff room of Bio-Site 66 and it was estimated to be consumed by 15-20 researchers and doctors on at least a weekly basis.

I don't believe that this would happen. Just because a bunch of D-Class day it tastes good doesn't mean that we allow it to be eaten; they would test for long term effects.

The reproduction event causes surprisingly little physical harm to the subject but may cause emotional trauma, therefore subjects are to be given Class C amnestics after treatment for any minor injuries sustained.

This paragraph feels out of place.

Overall, this was very meh to me, and it felt like an old article. I know this was to Dmatix's challenge, but I also think that the overall SCP could have been less disconnected in what it does.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Woops, it was meant to be changed to Euclid way back, perhaps I accidentally posted a slightly older version. I'll have yet another look through it but it's the first thing I've written for as long as I can remember so my grammar is far from top-notch.
e. as for the idea I should explain that this is a result of Dmatix's "random word generator" challenge here http://www.scp-wiki.net/forum/t-704995/a-new-challenge-appears-dmatix-five-word-extravaganza

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You've got some grammatical errors re: run-on sentences. I fixed a few, but you might want to give this a once-over to make sure it's mechanically sound.

Otherwise, this is executed well enough, but I can't really get excited about it. Aside from the fact that the containment procedures seem specific enough to warrant a Euclid, the idea itself didn't grab me. So, neutrality for the moment.
